issues.body: The new research record hard-codes percentages, millisecond timings, and a 3–8x penalty derived from a native shared-work-box run. Calling them advisory does not cure the repository's explicit prohibition on hard-coded performance numbers and its rule that work-box timings are non-canonical. These numbers are also used to justify the public feature in README/SKILL prose, so this is not merely an incidental lab note.
issues.fix_hint: Remove the numeric table and all concrete percentages, timings, and multiplier claims from committed Markdown. Keep only a qualitative, clearly non-canonical observation and point to a generated/git-ignored benchmark envelope or the future canonical quiet-box artifact for quantitative evidence; soften dependent public prose accordingly until that evidence exists.
issues.title: Non-canonical work-box performance numbers are committed as durable claims

issues.body: The comments say CI builds, clippies, and tests EVERY feature state, but the matrix covers default, shacl-af alone, and stateful alone. It never covers shacl-af+stateful together, even though both public opt-in features can be enabled simultaneously and stateful validation inherits sparq-shacl behavior under shacl-af.
issues.fix_hint: Either add build/clippy/wasm-pack coverage for --features shacl-af,stateful (preferably using --all-features where appropriate), or change all 'every feature state' claims to accurately describe the individually tested states and explain why the combination is unnecessary.
issues.title: CI claims every feature state but omits the combined feature state

summary: The stateful handle is narrowly implemented and its wasm tests exercise the real exported parse/validate path, but the change cannot be approved because it commits session/work-box performance measurements as durable numeric claims. Repository policy explicitly treats those measurements as non-canonical and forbids hard-coded performance numbers in Markdown. The CI/documentation also overstates feature-combination coverage.
